info.parenttitle: A3.4 Introduction to electronic systems (HL only)

markdown:
## Analogue Signals


1. **Analogue signals** are **continuous** and **vary smoothly** over time.
2. They represent **real-world phenomena** like sound, light, and temperature.
3. **Continuous Variation**: Unlike digital signals, which are discrete, analogue signals change **gradually**.

<callout type="analogy">* Think of an analogue signal as a **smooth wave **in the ocean, continuously rising and falling. 
* In contrast, a digital signal is like a series of **stepped terraces**, with distinct levels and no gradual transitions.

![Smooth waves in the ocean (pexels)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/a4b47f0e914cddf66795accf69832aa1ce37b1c9-6000x4000.png)</callout>

### Key Characteristics of Analogue Systems


#### Voltage (V)


<definition term="Voltage">The **electromotive force** from a power source that **pushes electrons through a circuit**, often described as **electric pressure**. Measured in **volts (V)**</definition>

![A microphone converts sound waves into an analogue voltage signal (AVSL)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5224a715be6d81f4902f1900d5cddecd41380398-3752x1064.png)

#### Current (I)


<definition term="Current ">The **flow of electric charge** through a circuit, caused by the **movement of electrically charged particles**, such as electrons. Measured in **amperes (A)**</definition>

![The current flowing through a speaker coil to produce sound (sound certified)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/3144a9217287f968c5f681c65f2c3767368b9ad8-788x420.png)

#### Resistance (R)


<definition term="Resistance">The **opposition to the flow of electrons** in an electrical circuit. It can be calculated using **Ohm’s Law**, which relates **resistance (R)**, **voltage (V)**, and **current (I)**: R=V/I. Measured in **ohms (Ω)**</definition>

![Resistors in a circuit control the current to prevent damage](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/f4edc2b8a36910fb1d269683fdf413c810c18eeb-1000x1000.png)

#### Frequency (f)


<definition term="Frequency">The **rate at which an electrical signal alternates** between positive and negative per second, measured in **hertz (Hz)**.</definition>

![The frequency of a radio signal determines the station you tune into (Britannica)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/9cecce3cc8b4309c319a67566fed7dd3f1de4ea7-1920x1080.png)

#### Power (P)


<definition term="Power">**Power** is the rate at which energy is transferred or work is done.</definition>

![The power consumed by an amplifier to drive a speaker (CHT Solutions)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/ea7a15e82a4575c832a0fb24941dffe714515ddb-1400x787.png)

<callout type="note">* Analogue systems are often described using **sine waves**, which represent **pure **frequencies. 
* These waves are characterized by their **amplitude **(height) and **frequency **(number of cycles per second).

![Sine wave.](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/c7c430261c6cb059fc4e821409f50d207d0d7540-2028x1188.png)</callout>

### SI Units and Multipliers


Understanding the **International System of Units (SI)** is crucial for working with analogue systems.

  1. **Base Units**:
      1. **Voltage**: Volt (V)
    2. **Current**: Ampere (A)
    3. **Resistance**: Ohm (Ω)
    4. **Frequency**: Hertz (Hz)
    5. **Power**: Watt (W)
  2. **SI Multipliers**:
      1. **pico (p)**: $10^{-12}$
    2. **nano (n)**: $10^{-9}$
    3. **micro (μ)**: $10^{-6}$
    4. **milli (m)**: $10^{-3}$
    5. **kilo (k)**: $10^{3}$
    6. **mega (M)**: $10^{6}$
    7. **giga (G)**: $10^{9}$
    8. **tera (T)**: $10^{12}$

<callout type="tip">When working with analogue systems, always use **SI units **and **multipliers **to ensure **accuracy **and **consistency **in calculations.</callout>

### Applications of Analogue Systems


1. **Audio Processing**: Microphones and speakers use analogue signals to capture and reproduce sound.
2. **Radio Transmission**: Analogue signals carry information over radio waves, modulating frequency or amplitude.
3. **Temperature Sensors**: Thermocouples produce analogue voltage signals proportional to temperature changes.

### Advantages & Disadvantages of Analogue Systems


| Advantages | Disadvantages |
| --- | --- |
| Noise Resistance: Binary signals are less affected by interference | Loss of Detail: Quantization can lead to a loss of subtle information |
| Easy Storage and Processing: Data can be compressed, encrypted, and transmitted efficiently | Complexity: Requires conversion from analogue to digital and vice versa |

<callout type="warning">* Don't assume that **analogue systems **are **obsolete**. 
* While digital technology is **prevalent**, analogue systems remain **essential **in applications requiring **high fidelity **and **natural **signal representation.</callout>
